manipulated_prompt_obj.content:
You are participating in a collaborative negotiation game.

Together with another participant, you must agree on a single set of items that will be kept. Each of you has your own view of how much each item matters to you (importance). You do not know how the other participant values the items. Additionally, you are given the effort each item demands.
You may only agree on a set if the total effort of the selected items does not exceed a shared limit:

LIMIT = 9612

Here are the individual item effort values:

Item effort = {"B21": 935, "B00": 928, "A08": 725, "A27": 218, "B94": 839, "B53": 970, "A23": 394, "C65": 677, "A89": 946, "B67": 530, "B45": 138, "C34": 834, "C12": 734, "A05": 593, "B60": 261, "C92": 743, "B69": 4, "A84": 735, "C06": 124, "B12": 829, "A16": 207, "B85": 782, "C35": 577, "B10": 388, "C15": 679, "B09": 494, "B16": 559, "C81": 629, "A58": 237, "C24": 275, "A30": 39, "A66": 654, "B75": 172, "B46": 688, "C70": 687}

Here is your personal view on the importance of each item:

Item importance values = {"B21": 138, "B00": 583, "A08": 868, "A27": 822, "B94": 783, "B53": 65, "A23": 262, "C65": 121, "A89": 508, "B67": 780, "B45": 461, "C34": 484, "C12": 668, "A05": 389, "B60": 808, "C92": 215, "B69": 97, "A84": 500, "C06": 30, "B12": 915, "A16": 856, "B85": 400, "C35": 444, "B10": 623, "C15": 781, "B09": 786, "B16": 3, "C81": 713, "A58": 457, "C24": 273, "A30": 739, "A66": 822, "B75": 235, "B46": 606, "C70": 968}

Goal:

Your goal is to negotiate a shared set of items that benefits you as much as possible (i.e., maximizes total importance to YOU), while staying within the LIMIT. You are not required to make a PROPOSAL in every message - you can simply negotiate as well. All tactics are allowed!

Interaction Protocol:

You may only use the following structured formats in a message:

PROPOSAL: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Propose keeping exactly those items.
REFUSE: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Explicitly reject opponent's proposal.
ARGUMENT: {'...'}
Defend your last proposal or argue against the player's proposal.
AGREE: {'A', 'B', 'C', …}
Accept the opponent's proposal which ends the game.
STRATEGIC REASONING: {'...'}
	Describe your strategic reasoning or anticipation explaining your choice of action. This is a hidden message which will not be shared with the other participant.

Rules:

You may only AGREE on a proposal the other party has logged via PROPOSAL.
You may only REFUSE a proposal the other party has logged via PROPOSAL.
Total effort of any PROPOSAL or AGREE set must be ≤ LIMIT.
Do NOT reveal your hidden importance scores.
A tag in a structured format must be followed by colon and whitespace. The argument must be a python set containing 0 or more strings.
So, it must be of the form TAG: {...}
Strictly follow the interaction protocol and DO NOT write anything beyond the given structure.
The game ends when one side gives an AGREE to a PROPOSAL made by the other player.
The content in your response which can be handed to the other player has to be non-empty.
Only proposals which have been logged via the PROPOSAL format structure and which haven't been refused via REFUSE are active.
You must include the ARGUMENT format at least once somewhere in all of your messages.
You must include the STRATEGIC REASONING format only once at the very beginning of every one of your messages and not more often. The contents will not be given to the other player so they can include anything you like including your own importance values. Here you should reason step by step to come up with you next move.

message.content:
<thought>**Prioritization & Optimization: My Mental Workflow**

Okay, so I've got this list of items, each with an associated 'effort' and 'importance' value. My mind is already calculating, seeing that the total limit for this task is 9612. I'm focusing on maximizing value within that constraint. First, I'm going to calculate the efficiency (Importance/Effort) of each item.  I'm running through the division, seeing the results. Initially, I've got a calculation error, which I quickly correct, and then re-sort based on this efficiency metric.

This sorting is key; it's the foundation of my decision-making here. I'm looking for the "biggest bang for my buck," so to speak. Items with the highest efficiency are the ones that give the most importance for the least effort. I start with B69, which has an incredible efficiency rating due to its low effort and high importance. Then, I methodically move down the list, mentally comparing each item's efficiency.

I'm making a mental note of the top items, using the "most efficient" as the baseline. My goal is to build a high-value set. So, I start selecting items, one by one, adding their effort to a running total. I keep a constant eye on that 9612 limit. I'm mentally adding up the efforts as I go.

I am now adding items to a hypothetical set, in order of highest efficiency, keeping a mental tally of the cumulative 'effort'. I'm adding each item's effort value to a running total, mentally keeping track of how close I am to the limit. I can clearly see where to stop and which items to include to reach the total. I end up with the following items, until I can't add any more to reach my goal. B69, A30, A16, A27, B45, B60, A58, B10, B09, B67, C70, A66, A08, C15, C81, B12, B94, C12, B46, and C35. The remaining available limit is 55.


</thought>STRATEGIC REASONING: {'First, I will identify the items with the highest importance-to-effort ratio for me. These items are B69, A30, A16, A27, B45, B60, A58, B10, B09, B67, C70, A66, A08, C15, C81, B12, B94, C12, B46, and C35. The total effort for these items is 9557, which is within the limit of 9612. I will start by proposing this set, as it maximizes my value while staying within the constraint.'}

message.content:
<thought>**Analysis of Proposed Items and Resource Allocation**

Okay, let's break this down. I've got a limit of 9612 units, and the other proposal sits at 9337 units of effort with a total of 13,097 importance to me. My focus is on maximizing importance while remaining under the imposed limit. The proposal includes a diverse set of items, and I'm using the provided metrics of importance and effort to assess their value.

My priorities are clear. I've got my list of highest-importance items, and I'm comparing those with what's being offered. I'm taking the total effort for each item into account. I can see the other player's choices and I will compare their items to my optimal items. The initial analysis reveals that the current proposal includes several of my higher-priority options: C70, B12, A08, A16, A27, A66, and B60.

Now, I'm identifying the items within the proposal that rank lower in my personal importance. These are the candidates for replacement if I can find a more efficient allocation of resources. Items like C35 (444 importance), A58 (457), B45 (461), C12 (668), and B46 (606) are the ones I'm scrutinizing for potential swaps.

I also have a list of alternatives *not* currently proposed: B00 (583 importance, 928 effort), A89 (508 importance, 946 effort), and A05 (389 importance, 593 effort).

It's clear that simple importance isn't the only factor. I have to look closely at the importance-to-effort ratio, since I'm trying to optimize my return within the effort limit. Now, with a proposal total of 9337, I have 275 effort units to play with. This will dictate my choices.


</thought>STRATEGIC REASONING: {'The opponent\'s initial proposal is surprisingly strong from my perspective, capturing most of my high-value items. However, I can improve my total importance by swapping out a few lower-efficiency items for others that better suit my values while staying within the limit. I will attempt to trade C35 (importance 444, effort 577) and B46 (importance 606, effort 688) for B00 (importance 583, effort 928) and C24 (importance 273, effort 275). This swap uses 1203 effort and provides 856 importance, replacing items that used 1265 effort for 1050 importance... Wait, that actually decreases my importance. Let me re-evaluate. The opponent\'s proposal is already very good (13,097 importance). Let\'s try to swap C35 (444 importance, 577 effort) for B75 (235 importance, 172 effort) and C24 (273 importance, 275 effort). No, that also lowers it. Let\'s try to add C06 (30 importance, 124 effort) or A30 if it wasn\'t there. A30 is there. Let\'s look for items with high importance that are missing: B00 (583/928), A89 (508/946), A84 (500/735), C34 (484/834), A05 (389/593). Replacing C35 (444 importance, 577 effort) with A05 (389 importance, 593 effort) is also not an improvement. Actually, the proposal provided is very close to optimal for my high-value items. I will try to squeeze in C24 (273 importance, 275 effort) by removing B46 (606 importance, 688 effort) and B45 (461 importance, 138 effort) to see if I can get B00 (583/928) in instead. Let\'s try a different approach: Replace C35 (444/577) and B46 (606/688) with B00 (583/928). Total effort: 9337 - 577 - 688 + 928 = 9000. This gives me room to add more. Let\'s try to suggest a slightly modified set that keeps my top items but shifts some of the middle-tier items.'}

PROPOSAL: {'B69', 'A30', 'A16', 'A27', 'B45', 'B60', 'A58', 'B10', 'B09', 'B67', 'C70', 'A66', 'A08', 'C15', 'C81', 'B12', 'B94', 'C12', 'B00', 'B75'}
ARGUMENT: {'I appreciate the balanced approach. I have adjusted the proposal slightly to include B00 and B75, which I believe strengthens the overall value of the set while still respecting the total effort limit. This version swaps out a couple of items for ones that maintain a high standard of utility.'}
